discovery: ACTION6 (CLICK) is pair-cycle-only with no coordinate argument. Prior sweeps treated it as click(x,y), wasting 28 actions with 0 state changes. On L1 (single pair), CLICK is a no-op.
evidence: experience/sk48.json shows action_tries[6]=28, action_changes[6]=0. Confirmed in-game: STEP 11 CLICK -> NOTHING CHANGED.
training_signal: Gemma should learn: CLICK cycles which rail pair is selected, not where to click. On single-pair levels, skip CLICK entirely.

discovery: Rail extension adds 1 segment per ACTION4 (RIGHT). Each extension produces ~12px visual change. Extension is blocked at the board right edge (~5 segments max from head to edge). Blocked extension produces 0-1px change.
evidence: STEPs 1-4: 12px each. STEP 7 at max: 1px. STEP 9 at max after slide: NOTHING CHANGED.
training_signal: Gemma should learn: count extensions from head to edge to know max rail length. Blocked = no animation, tiny/zero pixel change.

discovery: Perpendicular slide (ACTION1 UP / ACTION2 DOWN) moves the entire rail vertically when a track is adjacent. Produces ~96-192px change depending on rail length. Track sprites are at cols 13-14 on the left edge of the board.
evidence: STEP 5 UP: 248px (rail + target push). STEP 6 DOWN: 192px (rail only). Short rail slides: ~96px.
training_signal: Gemma should learn: UP/DOWN with track adjacent = slide. Pixel change correlates with rail length. Short rail = clean slide, long rail = may push adjacent objects.

discovery: Retraction (ACTION3 LEFT) removes the segment nearest the head and shifts all remaining segments leftward by 1 tile. Produces ~12-13px change for empty segments, ~36px when an embedded target shifts with the retraction.
evidence: STEP 10: 13px (empty retract). STEP 43: 36px (retract with embedded GREEN target, GREEN moved from cols 42-45 to 36-39).
training_signal: Gemma should learn: retract = pull. Embedded targets move with retraction. Retract shortens rail to enable slides. Retract pixel change > normal = target is moving.

discovery: CRITICAL: Targets are NOT solid to the rail. When the rail extends so that a segment sprite overlaps a target sprite (same row + column range), the rail PASSES THROUGH the target. The target becomes embedded in the rail's stripe pattern. Visual signature: 3-frame animation (vs normal 2-frame) and reduced pixel change (4-9px vs normal 12px).
evidence: STEP 42 at GREEN's row: 3 frames, 4px. STEP at RED's row: 3 frames, 8px. Grid shows target color values (8/9/14) interleaved with segment stripe (1/2) at the embed position.
training_signal: Gemma should learn: 3-frame animation on extension = target interaction. Targets are pass-through, not walls. This is the core mechanic of sk48. The rail 'visits' targets by passing through them.

discovery: Slide behavior depends on rail length. SHORT rail (1-3 segments) slides freely without pushing targets. LONG rail (5 segments reaching target column cols 41-46) pushes any vertically-adjacent target stack during UP slides. DOWN slides never push targets (nothing below to push). This asymmetry enables decoupling: slide UP (push stack), slide DOWN (rail moves alone, stack stays).
evidence: Pristine with 1 seg: UP slide = 97px (no push). Extended to 5 segs: UP slide = 249px (push). DOWN after push: 192px (rail only, targets stay at pushed position). Subsequent UP from decoupled: 96-97px (rail alone in gap).
training_signal: Gemma should learn: rail length determines slide side-effects. Retract before sliding to avoid unintended target pushes. Decouple = UP then DOWN to separate rail from target stack.

discovery: PALETTE LABELS IN MECHANICS DOC ARE WRONG. Doc says color indices 8=grey, 9=maroon, 14=yellow. Actual rendered colors (verified against human game UI): 8=RED, 9=BLUE, 14=GREEN. The doc was likely written under the pre-4/6 palette that was fixed in dp-web4/sage-arc-collab#1. Static reference screenshot game-screenshots/sk48_L1.png also uses the old palette.
evidence: Compared raw grid (numpy color indices) to human game screenshot provided by Andy. Grid index 8 at target position renders as RED in human UI, not grey. Same for 9=BLUE (not maroon) and 14=GREEN (not yellow).
training_signal: Gemma should learn: do not trust color NAMES in mechanics docs. Use visual perception to identify colors. The index-to-RGB mapping may differ from documentation.

hypothesis: Segments change color when passing through target positions
result: WRONG. Segments stay as stripe pattern (1/2 alternating). Targets stay as colored blocks (8/9/14). They coexist at the same grid position — the target is embedded BETWEEN segment stripes, not absorbed.
what_i_would_do_differently: Look at the lower reference rail more carefully from the start. The interleaved stripe+target pattern is VISIBLE in the lower rail — it shows what the embed looks like before you even try it.

hypothesis: Sliding the rail to a target's row and extending would push the target horizontally off the board edge
result: WRONG. Targets are not solid to the rail. The rail passes through targets, not pushes them. The 'push' mechanic only works during perpendicular slides (vertical movement) when sprites are adjacent, not during extension (horizontal movement).
what_i_would_do_differently: Test extension INTO a target position earlier. I avoided it because I assumed targets were walls. The 'are objects solid?' question from Andy was the key assumption-challenger.

hypothesis: The win condition requires targets to be physically positioned at specific segment positions (P1=RED, P2=GREEN, P3=BLUE) via retract-pull
result: PARTIALLY WRONG. Retract-pull does physically move targets leftward, but the win condition triggers on VISITING all targets (extending through each at their row), not on final positional arrangement. L1 win fired on the 3rd extension at BLUE's row — before any retraction to position targets.
what_i_would_do_differently: Test the simplest win hypothesis first: does visiting (extending through) each target trigger win? I over-complicated the model by assuming positional matching was required.

solve_method: Extend to visit each target at its row, retract to shorten rail between visits, slide to next target row. Win triggers when all targets have been visited (extended through).
clean_solve_sequence: From pristine: slide UP x3 to RED row, extend x4 (visit RED), retract x4, slide DOWN x2 to GREEN row, extend x4 (visit GREEN), retract x3, slide UP x1 to BLUE row, extend x3 (visit BLUE, WIN)
key_insight: Targets are non-solid — the rail passes through them. The win condition is 'visit all targets' not 'arrange targets in a pattern'. The lower reference shows what targets look like when visited, not a positional template to reproduce.
human_coaching_moments: 'Are the objects solid?' — challenged assumption that targets are walls
'Can the rail do more than one thing?' — led to discovering slide asymmetry (up pushes, down doesn't)
'Is there a way to pull them?' — led to retract-as-pull discovery
'Think like a crow' — reframed from engineering to experimental probing
'MYST patterns' — reframed from simultaneous state to sequential visits

discovery: WIN CONDITION IS NOT VISITATION. The actual win check (gvtmoopqgy) compares target colors at segment positions between paired upper/lower rails, index by index. vjfbwggsd[head] is built by scanning segments left-to-right and collecting targets at each (x,y). Win = all corresponding indices match in color.
evidence: Source code analysis of gvtmoopqgy() in sk48.py lines 844-861. L0 verified: win fires when 3 targets at correct segment positions match ref [8,14,9].
training_signal: The L1 'visit all targets' finding was correct for L1 but not the general mechanism. Targets must be at segment positions with correct color ORDER matching the lower reference rail.

discovery: PERPENDICULAR PINNING: targets at positions with a perpendicular rail segment cannot be pushed in the extension direction. At (29,y) where vertical segments exist (y=0-24), horizontal extension passes through the target without moving it. Retraction also cannot pull such targets. The target stays fixed — the horizontal segment coexists at the same position.
evidence: bnrdrdiakd source analysis: pptqisyill finds perpendicular segment, (dir_x==0)!=(move_x==0) returns False. Target stays at position. Verified empirically: extending to x=29 at rows 6-24 embeds target (appears in vjfbwggsd) but doesn't move it.
training_signal: For levels with vertical rails, targets at the intersection are PINNED. Must push them OFF the perpendicular rail via parallel movement (slides) before they can be pulled/positioned.

discovery: PUSHING PINNED TARGETS VIA SLIDES: A target pinned at (29,24) by the vertical rail CAN be pushed DOWN (parallel to vert rail) by sliding DOWN from y=18 with seg at x=29. The push succeeds because the direction is parallel to the pinning segment. Required: clear the destination (29,30) first by moving any target there out of the way.
evidence: Tested empirically: after clearing (29,30), sliding DOWN from y=18 with 5 segs pushes target(14) from (29,24) to (29,30). Verified by comparing target positions before/after.
training_signal: Perpendicular-pinned targets can be freed by pushing parallel to the pinning rail. This requires clearing the destination first.

discovery: EXTENSION PUSH AT EDGE: When the play area right edge blocks further extension (last segment can't move right), extension is blocked entirely. Any target at the edge position STAYS — it cannot be pushed further. This is the KEY to the L0 technique: extend to max, target at edge stays, retract to pull it back.
evidence: L0 source: qzvlbxkjgk checks bounds for last segment. L0 play_edge=47: target at x=41 stays when extending to 6 segs. L2 play_edge=53: target pushed to x=47 stays at 8 segs.
training_signal: The extend-to-edge technique: extend fully so target reaches the rightmost possible position, then retract to pull it LEFT. Retraction pulls ALL targets on the rail LEFT by one cell each.

solve_method: UNSOLVED. L2 phases 1-3 complete (push stack down, clear path, push pinned target). Phase 4 (final arrangement of 4 targets in ref order [8,12,9,14] at distinct x on same row) remains the blocker.
key_insight: L2 has a vertical rail at x=29 that pins upper targets. Must push targets below vert rail (y>24) then arrange. The extend-retract technique from L0 works for collecting targets at the board edge, but arranging 4 targets in a specific non-trivial order [8,12,9,14] at one row requires careful sequencing to avoid chain-push collisions.
blockers: Targets at x=29 on different rows block each other during slides with long rail
Extension pushes ALL on-row targets right, making relative ordering dependent on collection sequence
First-collected target ends up leftmost after retraction chain; need [8,12,9,14] left-to-right

discovery: L2 HAS THREE RAILS, not two. A VERTICAL rail at head (29,0) with 5 segs to (29,24) exists in game.mwfajkguqx but is NOT in xpmcmtbcv (unpaired). It has sys_click tag but CLICK handler only activates sprites already in xpmcmtbcv pairs, so this vertical rail is UNSELECTABLE -- effectively static scenery. Its only role is to create perpendicular pin segments at x=29, y=0..24. This is the root cause of the 'x=29 pinning' observed empirically.
evidence: game.mwfajkguqx has 3 keys on L2: (5,42) horizontal paired, (17,56) horizontal paired, (29,0) vertical UNPAIRED. xpmcmtbcv has only 1 entry: (5,42)->(17,56). CLICK at (29,0) or (30,1) leaves vzvypfsnt unchanged.
training_signal: When analyzing sk48 levels, dump game.mwfajkguqx (all rails) AND game.xpmcmtbcv (pairs) separately. Unpaired rails in mwfajkguqx function as pin scenery.

discovery: RETRACTION-DRAG below pin row: a target at (29,y>24) can be moved left off x=29 via extend-retract cycle. Example verified: from rail at y=30 with 3 segs (5..17), extend 5x to reach 8 segs (5..47) pushing target(9) from (29,30) to (47,30). Retract 4x -> 4 segs (5..23), target(9) now at (23,30). Net: target moved from x=29 to x=23, 9 actions total (5 ext + 4 retract).
evidence: Empirical trace via bnrdrdiakd in the real engine, logged step-by-step state.
training_signal: To move a non-pinned target horizontally, use extend-to-edge then retract. Target ends up at rail's NEW rightmost segment after retractions.

hypothesis: Once a target is embedded in a rail segment, it can be detached by sliding the rail to a different row (target stays)
result: WRONG. Embedded target always moves with its segment on slides. The only way to separate is to move the rail horizontally PAST the target's position so no segment lives at the target's (x,y) anymore. Retracting past moves the target too (segment drags it). Extending past from the right side isn't possible because head is on the left (rot=0). The target only truly detaches when a RIGHTWARD extension pushes segments past it while the target is PINNED (can't move). That's how L1 visited targets at the edge.
what_i_would_do_differently: Accept that below the pin row, targets are INSEPARABLE from whichever segment last touched them. This forces a particular choreography: you can only position ONE target per 'gesture', and the rail ends up where the target goes.

solve_method: STILL UNSOLVED. Core blocker: target(14) is pinned at (29,24) and cannot be freed. Slide-chain-down moves the whole column together, and the bottom target (12) blocks at y=42 (can't go to y=48 OOB) after dn3, stranding target(14) at the last pinned row (29,24). Cannot un-pin target(14) without separating it from the column first, which requires horizontal displacement of a lower target. But all 4 targets start at y<=24 where they're ALL pinned -- no horizontal moves available. Catch-22.
key_insight: L2 is not just a search problem -- there may be a fundamental constraint: target(14) cannot be positioned right of target(9) unless it escapes the pin. The reference order [8,12,9,14] requires 14 to be rightmost. Either (a) the intended solve uses the LOWER rail somehow (not achievable via CLICK since the lower rail is the paired reference, not a separate controllable), (b) there's a mechanic we're still missing, or (c) L2 is solvable with 14 at x=29 and other targets to the right (9 at x=35, 12 at x=41, 8 at x=47 is wrong order anyway).
blockers: Target 14 pinned at (29,24) and cannot be displaced without moving target 12 first, which itself cannot be displaced until moved below y=24, which requires pushing it past the bottom target (12) that blocks the chain at y=48.
All four targets initially at x=29 under the pin means no horizontal manipulation possible until chain-down.
